revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:磯部の御神田]]
'''Isobe-no-Omita''' (磯部の御神田) is a traditional folk performing art in the form of {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|qid=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} associated with {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|en|Izawa-no-miya|qid=Q10885171|lt=Izawa-no-miya|1=Izawa-no-miya}}, located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|en|Shima, Mie|qid=Q338009|lt=Shima, Mie|1=Shima, Mie}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The group responsible for preserving this tradition is the Isobe-no-Omita Service Association. It was selected as an [[:en:Important_Intangible_Folk_Cultural_Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] for the measures to create records of intangible cultural properties in 1973, and designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property in 1990. It has also been selected as one of the "Tōkai Scenic 100 Selections" by the Tōkai Regional Agricultural Administration Bureau.<ref name="mbe">[[Mie Prefectural Board of Education]] "[http://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/bunkazai/da/item/detail.asp?wag=7&prePageNum=2&mngnum=0000957 Protecting and Preserving Mie’s Cultural Properties Database/Isobe-no-Omita]" (Accessed December 18, 2013)</ref><ref>{{Cite web|publisher=Tōkai Regional Agricultural Administration Bureau|url=http://www.maff.go.jp/tokai/noson/shinko/hyaku/100sen/mie/index.html|title=Isobe-no-Omita|accessdate=December 1, 2011}}</ref>
[[File:Izawa-mitaue_03.JPG|thumb|300px|Izawa-no-Miya Rice Planting Festival]]
The performance takes place annually on June 24 during the ''Izawa-no-Miya Rice Planting Festival''. The festival itself is also referred to as "Omita." The date of June 24 is linked to the legend of the "Seven Sharks," where seven sharks are said to have swum up the Noga River during {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}'s journey, guiding her to the site of Izawa-no-Miya.<ref name="海博6">Sea Museum, Ishihara (1996) p.16</ref> According to tradition, seven sharks visit the shrine annually, and nearby fishermen refrain from fishing on this day.<ref name="海博6"/> ...
== References ==
